reference: GATE CS — Databases / Constraints and Normalization

map.condition: Checking if a relation is in 1NF
map.use: \text{All attributes are atomic (no repeating groups or multi-valued attributes)}

map.condition: Checking if a relation is in 2NF
map.use: \text{In 1NF} \land \text{No partial dependency: every non-prime attribute fully depends on every candidate key}

map.condition: Checking if a relation is in 3NF
map.use: \text{In 2NF} \land \text{No transitive dependency: } X \to A \Rightarrow X \text{ is superkey or } A \text{ is prime}

map.condition: Checking if a relation is in BCNF
map.use: \forall X \to Y \text{ (non-trivial): } X \text{ must be a superkey}

map.condition: Checking if a relation is in 4NF
map.use: \text{In BCNF} \land \forall X \twoheadrightarrow Y \text{ (non-trivial): } X \text{ must be a superkey}

formulas.name: Functional Dependency Definition
formulas.latex: X \to Y \iff \forall t_1, t_2 \in r,\; t_1[X] = t_2[X] \Rightarrow t_1[Y] = t_2[Y]
formulas.plain: X -> Y iff for all tuples t1, t2 in r: t1[X] = t2[X] implies t1[Y] = t2[Y]
formulas.whenToUse: Verifying whether Y is functionally determined by X in a given relation instance.

terms.means: determinant attribute set
dependent attribute set
any two tuples in the relation r

formulas.trap: A single relation instance satisfying X -> Y does not prove the FD holds; it must hold for ALL valid instances.

formulas.name: Armstrong's Axiom — Reflexivity
formulas.latex: Y \subseteq X \Rightarrow X \to Y
formulas.plain: If Y is a subset of X, then X -> Y (trivial FD)
formulas.whenToUse: Deriving trivial functional dependencies; always true.

terms.means: attribute sets with Y contained in X

formulas.trap: Trivial FDs (where Y ⊆ X) are always satisfied and do not constrain the data.

formulas.name: Armstrong's Axiom — Augmentation
formulas.latex: X \to Y \Rightarrow XZ \to YZ
formulas.plain: If X -> Y, then XZ -> YZ (adding Z to both sides)
formulas.whenToUse: Extending an existing FD by appending the same attributes to both sides.

terms.means: disjoint or overlapping attribute sets

formulas.trap: Z can overlap with X or Y — only additional attributes matter for new derivations.

formulas.name: Armstrong's Axiom — Transitivity
formulas.latex: X \to Y \land Y \to Z \Rightarrow X \to Z
formulas.plain: If X -> Y and Y -> Z, then X -> Z
formulas.whenToUse: Chaining functional dependencies to derive new ones.

terms.means: attribute sets forming a chain of dependencies

formulas.trap: Transitivity is the basis for transitive dependency violations in 3NF — do not confuse derived FD with direct FD.

formulas.name: Attribute Closure X+
formulas.latex: X^+ = \{ A \mid X \to A \text{ is derivable from } F \}
formulas.plain: X+ = {A | X -> A is derivable from F using Armstrong's axioms}
formulas.whenToUse: Finding all attributes determined by X; testing superkeys and candidate keys.

terms.means: closure of attribute set X under F
set of given functional dependencies

formulas.trap: If X+ = all attributes, then X is a superkey. If no proper subset of X has X+ = all attributes, X is a candidate key.

formulas.name: Union & Decomposition Rules (Derived)
formulas.latex: \text{Union: } X \to Y,\; X \to Z \Rightarrow X \to YZ \qquad \text{Decomp: } X \to YZ \Rightarrow X \to Y,\; X \to Z
formulas.plain: Union: X->Y and X->Z implies X->YZ. Decomposition: X->YZ implies X->Y and X->Z.
formulas.whenToUse: Splitting or merging the right-hand side of functional dependencies.

terms.means: attribute sets

formulas.trap: Decomposition works on the RHS but NOT the LHS: X->Y does NOT imply X\ minus\ A -> Y.

formulas.name: First Normal Form (1NF)
formulas.latex: \text{1NF: All attributes are atomic; no repeating groups or multi-valued attributes in any tuple}
formulas.plain: 1NF: Every attribute value is atomic (indivisible); no set-valued or composite attributes.
formulas.whenToUse: Baseline requirement for any relational table; needed before checking higher normal forms.

terms.means: cannot be further subdivided into meaningful sub-parts

formulas.trap: A relation can be in 1NF but still have redundancy via partial or transitive dependencies.

formulas.name: Second Normal Form (2NF)
formulas.latex: \text{2NF: In 1NF} \land \text{ No non-prime attribute is partially dependent on any candidate key}
formulas.plain: 2NF: In 1NF AND every non-prime attribute is fully functionally dependent on every candidate key (no partial dependency).
formulas.whenToUse: When a relation has a composite candidate key and you suspect some attributes depend only on part of the key.

terms.means: attribute not part of any candidate key
non-prime attribute depends on a proper subset of a candidate key

formulas.trap: 2NF only matters when there is a composite candidate key; with a single-attribute key a 1NF relation is automatically in 2NF.

formulas.name: Third Normal Form (3NF)
formulas.latex: \text{3NF: } \forall X \to A \text{ (non-trivial): } X \text{ is a superkey } \lor A \text{ is a prime attribute}
formulas.plain: 3NF: For every non-trivial FD X->A, either X is a superkey OR A is a prime attribute (member of some candidate key).
formulas.whenToUse: Eliminating transitive dependencies while still guaranteeing lossless-join and dependency-preserving decomposition.

terms.means: single attribute on RHS
attribute that belongs to at least one candidate key

formulas.trap: 3NF allows an FD X->A where A is prime even if X is not a superkey — this is the key difference from BCNF.

formulas.name: Boyce-Codd Normal Form (BCNF)
formulas.latex: \text{BCNF: } \forall X \to Y \text{ (non-trivial): } X \text{ must be a superkey}
formulas.plain: BCNF: For every non-trivial FD X->Y, X must be a superkey. Stricter than 3NF.
formulas.whenToUse: Eliminating all FD-based redundancy; use when 3NF is insufficient.

terms.means: attribute set whose closure contains all attributes of the relation

formulas.trap: BCNF decomposition always yields lossless join but may NOT preserve all functional dependencies — trade-off vs 3NF.

formulas.name: Fourth Normal Form (4NF)
formulas.latex: \text{4NF: In BCNF} \land \forall X \twoheadrightarrow Y \text{ (non-trivial, } Y \not\subseteq X\text{, } XY \neq R\text{): } X \text{ is a superkey}
formulas.plain: 4NF: In BCNF AND for every non-trivial MVD X ->> Y, X must be a superkey.
formulas.whenToUse: Removing multi-valued dependency redundancy after achieving BCNF.

terms.means: X multi-determines Y (MVD)

formulas.trap: Every FD is also an MVD, so 4NF is strictly stronger than BCNF.

formulas.name: Normal Form Containment Hierarchy
formulas.latex: \text{4NF} \subset \text{BCNF} \subset \text{3NF} \subset \text{2NF} \subset \text{1NF}
formulas.plain: 4NF is subset of BCNF is subset of 3NF is subset of 2NF is subset of 1NF
formulas.whenToUse: Quickly determining which normal forms a relation satisfies given it satisfies a higher one.

terms.means: strict containment; every relation in the left NF is also in the right NF

formulas.trap: The containment is strict: a relation in 3NF is not necessarily in BCNF.

formulas.name: Multivalued Dependency Definition
formulas.latex: X \twoheadrightarrow Y \iff \text{in any valid instance, the set of } Y\text{-values for a given }X\text{-value is independent of } Z = R - X - Y
formulas.plain: X ->> Y iff the set of Y values associated with any X value is independent of the Z values (Z = R minus X minus Y).
formulas.whenToUse: Identifying independent multi-valued facts stored together, causing 4NF violation.

terms.means: X multi-determines Y
remaining attributes R - X - Y

formulas.trap: An MVD X ->> Y is trivial if Y ⊆ X or XY = R; only non-trivial MVDs can cause 4NF violations.

formulas.name: FD Implies MVD (but not vice-versa)
formulas.latex: X \to Y \Rightarrow X \twoheadrightarrow Y \quad \text{(converse is false)}
formulas.plain: If X -> Y (FD) then X ->> Y (MVD). The reverse does not hold.
formulas.whenToUse: Reasoning about whether an FD is also an MVD; important for 4NF proofs.

terms.means: functional dependency
multivalued dependency

formulas.trap: A -> B and A -> C implies A ->> BC (as a combined MVD), but A ->> B and A ->> C does NOT imply A -> B or A -> C.

formulas.name: MVD Complementation Rule
formulas.latex: X \twoheadrightarrow Y \Rightarrow X \twoheadrightarrow (R - X - Y)
formulas.plain: If X ->> Y then X ->> (R minus X minus Y). MVDs always come in complementary pairs.
formulas.whenToUse: Deriving the complementary MVD; useful in 4NF decomposition.

terms.means: attributes of R not in X or Y

formulas.trap: Both Y and (R - X - Y) are independently multi-determined by X — this is the source of 4NF redundancy.

formulas.name: Lossless-Join Decomposition Condition
formulas.latex: R \to \{R_1, R_2\} \text{ is lossless} \iff (R_1 \cap R_2) \to R_1 \in F^+ \;\lor\; (R_1 \cap R_2) \to R_2 \in F^+
formulas.plain: Decomposition of R into R1 and R2 is lossless iff (R1 intersect R2) -> R1 OR (R1 intersect R2) -> R2 holds.
formulas.whenToUse: Verifying that a binary decomposition loses no information on natural join.

terms.means: common attributes of R1 and R2; must be a superkey of at least one part
closure of the set of FDs F

formulas.trap: Lossless-join and dependency-preservation are independent properties; a decomposition can have one without the other.

formulas.name: Dependency-Preserving Decomposition
formulas.latex: (F_1 \cup F_2 \cup \cdots \cup F_k)^+ = F^+ \quad \text{where } F_i = \pi_{R_i}(F)
formulas.plain: A decomposition {R1,...,Rk} is dependency-preserving if the union of projections of F onto each Ri has the same closure as F.
formulas.whenToUse: Checking whether all original FDs can be enforced without re-joining fragments.

terms.means: projection of F: FDs in F+ involving only attributes of Ri
full closure of all original FDs

formulas.trap: If decomposition is NOT dependency-preserving, a JOIN is needed to check violated FDs — at extra cost.

formulas.name: 3NF Decomposition Guarantees
formulas.latex: \text{3NF decomposition (synthesis): Lossless-join} \land \text{Dependency-preserving} \land \text{Each schema in 3NF}
formulas.plain: 3NF synthesis algorithm always yields a lossless, dependency-preserving decomposition with each schema in 3NF.
formulas.whenToUse: Choosing between 3NF and BCNF decomposition when dependency preservation is required.

terms.means: algorithm creating one schema per FD in minimal cover

formulas.trap: BCNF decomposition may NOT preserve all FDs; 3NF is preferred when dependency preservation is mandatory.

formulas.name: BCNF Decomposition Guarantees
formulas.latex: \text{BCNF decomposition: Lossless-join} \land \text{Each schema in BCNF} \;(\text{but dependency preservation NOT guaranteed})
formulas.plain: BCNF decomposition always yields a lossless-join decomposition with each schema in BCNF, but may not preserve all FDs.
formulas.whenToUse: When eliminating all FD-based redundancy is the priority over dependency preservation.

terms.means: an FD X->Y where X is not a superkey; split on this FD

formulas.trap: After BCNF decomposition, some FDs may only be enforceable by joining all fragments — an expensive operation.

formulas.name: Superkey vs Candidate Key
formulas.latex: \text{Superkey: } X^+ = R \qquad \text{Candidate Key: } X^+ = R \land \nexists\, Y \subsetneq X : Y^+ = R
formulas.plain: Superkey: closure of X = all attributes. Candidate Key: minimal superkey (no proper subset is also a superkey).
formulas.whenToUse: Finding candidate keys before checking normal forms or determining prime attributes.

terms.means: closure of attribute set X
set of all attributes in the relation schema

formulas.trap: A relation can have multiple candidate keys; every candidate key is a superkey but not every superkey is a candidate key.

formulas.name: Prime vs Non-Prime Attributes
formulas.latex: \text{Prime: } A \in \text{some candidate key} \qquad \text{Non-prime: } A \notin \text{any candidate key}
formulas.plain: Prime attribute: belongs to at least one candidate key. Non-prime: does not belong to any candidate key.
formulas.whenToUse: Classifying attributes when checking 2NF, 3NF, and BCNF conditions.

terms.means: a single attribute of the relation

formulas.trap: In 3NF, a dependency X -> A is allowed even if X is not a superkey, provided A is prime — BCNF removes this exception.

formulas.name: Minimal Cover (Canonical Cover)
formulas.latex: F_c: \text{RHS is single attribute} \land \text{No extraneous attribute on LHS} \land \text{No redundant FD}
formulas.plain: Minimal cover Fc of F: every FD has a single attribute RHS, no LHS attribute is extraneous, no FD is redundant.
formulas.whenToUse: Computing the minimal cover before applying 3NF synthesis decomposition.

terms.means: canonical cover / minimal cover of F
an attribute whose removal does not change the closure

formulas.trap: The minimal cover is not necessarily unique — different orderings can yield different but equally valid minimal covers.
